It was São Francisco that taught me.
Had I not worked there.
I managed it, and I also ran it.
Thanks for making the time, João. I've read your CV, so don't just read it back to me — tell me about yourself, and why you're the right person for this role.

I'm a signaling engineer with domain expertise in CBTC and hands-on experience from factory tests to live running. It was the São Francisco commissioning that gave me my first real exposure to international standards — and there I took ownership of the supplier interface.
Good — that's a case, not a CV. Walk me through what you actually did on that commissioning.

Not only did I manage the technical scope, but I also coordinated three overseas suppliers at once, as the technical liaison. I demonstrated initiative by proposing a risk register no one had asked for — and it caught two faults before they reached the track. That's measurable value, not just activity.
Every project has friction. Tell me about a conflict with one of those suppliers — and don't sand off the edges.

I'll take that head-on. One supplier filed a delay claim; our own approval records had a gap. I took ownership of that gap, put the dated evidence on the table, and we settled without prejudice to either side. Had I not owned it early, the dispute would have cost us months — so I now log every approval the same day.
Last one. Why an international role, and why now?

I'm not looking to leave a job — I'm pursuing an international role where my expertise adds the most measurable value. Had I not spent those years on live commissioning, I wouldn't have the judgment I bring now. I want to implement best practices across borders and contribute to the broader goals of a company that works the way this one does.
I'm a signaling engineer with domain expertise in CBTC and hands-on experience from factory tests to live running.
I'll take that head-on: here's what went wrong, and here's what I now do differently.
I'm pursuing an international role where I can add measurable value and contribute to the broader goals.

Tell me about yourself. Fifteen years in railway signaling, and I still start the same way, because a hiring manager is not asking for your life story, they are asking for your case. So I lead with what I am, not where I was born. I'm a signaling engineer with deep domain expertise in CBTC commissioning, and hands-on experience taking systems from the factory acceptance test all the way to live running on metro lines. That's the headline. Then I give it a spine, one project that proves it. It was the commissioning of a driverless line that gave me my first real exposure to international standards, and on that project I acted as the technical liaison between the operator and three overseas suppliers at once. Notice I don't just list tasks. I took ownership of the interface management, I demonstrated initiative when I proposed a risk register no one had asked for, and by the end that register had caught two faults before they reached the track. That's measurable value, and a hiring manager remembers the number, not the adjective. Then I close with why I'm in the room. Had I not spent those years on live commissioning, I wouldn't have the judgment I bring now, and that judgment is exactly what an international role rewards. I'm not looking to leave a job, I'm looking to add measurable value on a bigger stage, and to contribute to the broader goals of a company that works across borders. Keep it to ninety seconds. Make a compelling case, not a confession. And whatever you do, address the hard questions head-on, because the candidate who owns the difficult part is the one they trust with the easy part.

Three things before you walk into that interview, from someone who has coached a lot of engineers through exactly this door. First, they are not testing your English, they are testing whether you can make them confident. So stop translating your CV and start making a case. Every answer should quietly say I have done this, here is the proof, and here is what it will do for you. That is the difference between sounding rehearsed and sounding senior. Second, use the structures that carry authority, and you already have them, so use them on purpose. When you say not only did I manage the scope, but I also ran the supplier interface, the inversion makes the sentence lean forward, it sounds like leadership. When you say it was the São Francisco project that taught me international standards, the cleft puts the spotlight exactly where you want it. And when you say had I not worked on live commissioning, I wouldn't have this judgment, the conditional turns your past into the reason to hire you. These are not tricks, they are emphasis, and emphasis is what a nervous candidate forgets. Third, the hardest question is a gift. When they ask about a conflict with a supplier, or your greatest professional challenge, do not deflect and do not shrink. Address it head-on, take ownership of your part, and show what you changed because of it. The engineer who says here is what went wrong and here is what I now do differently is far stronger than the one with no scars. Lead with your expertise, prove it with one project, and let the difficult question be the moment you look most in control. That is how you turn forty-five minutes into an offer.
